reasoning: All three correctly diagnose the observational design, separate the relative-vs-absolute risk problem (naming concrete base-rate scenarios), surface confounding and reverse causation, and flag the unjustified drink-to-extract leap plus the seller's conflict of interest. B is strongest: it is the only one to anchor an explicit numerical prior (15-25% via the vitamin-E/beta-carotene/HRT graveyard outside view), update it to a stated 20-25% posterior, separately price the supplement under 10%, raise multiple-comparisons, and propose Mendelian randomization as a discriminating test, giving the best calibration and depth. A is tight, well-structured, and fully correct but stays qualitative with no probability estimates or base-rate history. C adds real external evidence (the 2023 meta-analysis showing ~4% per cup, far below the brand's 20%, and FDA disease-claim guidance) which is concrete and useful, but its cited specifics are unverifiable here and it offers less explicit uncertainty quantification than B, placing it third by a narrow margin.
